ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai-style burnt or chargrilled squid; closest parent 'squid food product' (FOODON_03302751) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers squids as a category; no specific code for grilled or charred squid preparation exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Thai-style grilled squid. SNOMED CT does not provide sufficient granularity for this prepared seafood dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant portion of Thai-style burnt squid is approximately 250 g, typically served whole or in large pieces with a dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Squid, mixed species, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175183)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Thai-style chargrilled squid exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. USDA SR Legacy cooked squid (FDC ID 175183) is used as the closest defensible analogue for the squid component. Sodium is adjusted upward to reflect typical Thai marinade contributions (fish sauce, oyster sauce) based on FAO/INFOODS SEA regional seasoning guidance. Values represent an estimated composite for the grilled, marinated preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021;114(5):1625–1632.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai-style burnt squid. Squid and other cephalopods are high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafoods. GI is estimated at 40 (low category) by analogy with other lean grilled seafood and mixed protein-carbohydrate dishes with fish sauce marinade, consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database classification of high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood preparations. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (40 × 7.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5) / 100 = 7.8 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and oyster sauce marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced fish sauce in the marinade, as a 250 g serving provides approximately 1550 mg sodium, exceeding 65% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai-style burnt squid has a low estimated GI of 40 and a moderate glycaemic load of approximately 7.8 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol content and high sodium from marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Squid is naturally high in cholesterol (approximately 233 mg per 100 g) and the Thai marinade adds significant sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size and frequency, though the low saturated fat content and presence of omega-3 polyunsaturated fats are partially mitigating factors.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai-style burnt squid, known in Thai as ปลาหมึกย่างไหม้ (Pla Muek Yang Mai), is a popular Southeast Asian grilled seafood dish in which whole squid or squid tubes are marinated in a mixture of fish sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, coriander root, and white pepper, then cooked over high-heat charcoal or open flame until the exterior is charred and slightly smoky. The 'burnt' or charred quality is intentional and prized, imparting a deep, smoky bitterness that contrasts with the sweet-savoury marinade. The texture is firm and slightly chewy with a caramelised outer crust. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai seafood restaurants and is typically accompanied by a spicy seafood dipping sauce (nam jim seafood) made from fresh chilli, garlic, lime juice, fish sauce, and palm sugar. Nutritionally, the dish is a high-protein, moderate-fat food with a low glycaemic index, making it suitable for most dietary patterns when sodium intake is managed. The high cholesterol content of squid warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-containing due to oyster sauce and is not suitable for those with shellfish or mollusc allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Thai grilled squid?
Is Thai squid high in sodium?
What does burnt squid taste like?
Is grilled squid good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai-style burnt squid exists in any primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked squid with sodium adjusted for Thai marinade contributions. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy squid entry (FDC ID 175183) and no reliable regional source provides this value for grilled squid. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy squid entry and is not available from the adapted sources used. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible for grilled seafood with no hydrogenated fat in the marinade, consistent with USDA SR Legacy data. Sodium of 620 mg per 100 g reflects an upward adjustment from the USDA baseline (~260 mg per 100 g for cooked squid) to account for fish sauce and oyster sauce marinade typical of Thai-style preparations, based on FAO/INFOODS SEA regional guidance. Carbohydrate of 7.8 g per 100 g reflects the marinade contribution (oyster sauce, sugar). The food_name has been normalised from the promotional menu name 'Ultimate Burnt Squid' to the canonical descriptor 'Burnt Squid (Thai-style Grilled Squid)'; the original menu name is preserved in aliases. GI estimated at 40 (low) by analogy with grilled high-protein seafood per University of Sydney GI Database classification principles.
